result: The sea at dusk looks like a fabric someone keeps rubbing between invisible fingers: every pass raises a different nap of color. Tonight it’s the shade of old steel struck once by moonlight, a bruise blue that wants to be black but hasn’t admitted it yet. I stand where the tide forgets itself and remember that my blood is roughly the same salinity as this water; we are cousins who lost touch. When I was small I believed the waves were rehearsing a message for me personally, and if I listened long enough the vowels would separate from the hissing consonants and I’d know my purpose. Instead, years later, I know only that the message is still being written, rewritten, erased byFoam. Somewhere out there a discarded bottle spins with a note I never sent: Dear Future, I am afraid of becoming a footnote in my own life. The irony is that the bottle will shatter first, its shards tumbled into sea glass, jewelry for a stranger. I take off my shoes because shoes lie; they pretend we can walk without leaving footprints. The sand is cold, volcanic, eager to swallow heat. Each receding wave pulls a little of me with it—skin cells, yesterday’s grief, the nickel I palmed instead of tossing into a fountain. I give it permission. What’s left behind is lighter, not cleaner, but light enough to imagine carrying into tomorrow without stooped shoulders. Somewhere a clock strikes nine, but the ocean keeps its own time, measured in salt and almosts.

message.content: The sea at dusk looks like a fabric someone keeps rubbing between invisible fingers: every pass raises a different nap of color. Tonight it’s the shade of old steel struck once by moonlight, a bruise blue that wants to be black but hasn’t admitted it yet. I stand where the tide forgets itself and remember that my blood is roughly the same salinity as this water; we are cousins who lost touch. When I was small I believed the waves were rehearsing a message for me personally, and if I listened long enough the vowels would separate from the hissing consonants and I’d know my purpose. Instead, years later, I know only that the message is still being written, rewritten, erased byFoam. Somewhere out there a discarded bottle spins with a note I never sent: Dear Future, I am afraid of becoming a footnote in my own life. The irony is that the bottle will shatter first, its shards tumbled into sea glass, jewelry for a stranger. I take off my shoes because shoes lie; they pretend we can walk without leaving footprints. The sand is cold, volcanic, eager to swallow heat. Each receding wave pulls a little of me with it—skin cells, yesterday’s grief, the nickel I palmed instead of tossing into a fountain. I give it permission. What’s left behind is lighter, not cleaner, but light enough to imagine carrying into tomorrow without stooped shoulders. Somewhere a clock strikes nine, but the ocean keeps its own time, measured in salt and almosts.
